notes.anchor: With Lyso of Patrae I have indeed an old guest-friendship
notes.note: Lyso was a prominent citizen of Patrae (Roman colony in Achaea, modern Patras), with whom Cicero had ties of hospitium --- the formal bond of reciprocal hospitality between Romans and provincials that carried social and legal obligations of mutual protection.

notes.anchor: because Lyso had been in our camp and under our protection
notes.note: Lyso had sided with the Pompeian (senatorial) cause before Caesar's victory in the civil war, making his property and safety precarious under Caesar's settlement; Cicero's phrase "our camp" (nostra causa nostrisque praesidiis) identifies Lyso as a fellow Pompeian sympathiser.

notes.anchor: we obtained from Caesar everything we wanted
notes.note: Caesar's clemency policy (clementia Caesaris) extended to provincial allies of his opponents; Cicero and other patrons successfully interceded to secure Lyso's property from confiscation, a result confirmed by a letter from Caesar to Servius.

notes.anchor: my client C. Maenius Gemellus, when in the calamity of his exile he had been made a citizen of Patrae, adopted under the laws of that city
notes.note: Maenius Gemellus was a Roman citizen exiled (perhaps under Sulla or by Caesarian decree) who took Patraean citizenship during his exile --- a Roman colonist city could absorb such persons. He then adopted Lyso's son under local law, entangling Roman inheritance rights with Greek civic adoption procedures, which explains why the case needed a proconsul's oversight.

notes.anchor: you will be of the same mind and the same disposition that I am
notes.note: Cicero closes with a standard recommendation formula predicting that the recommender's own high opinion of the beneficiary will be vindicated once the recipient knows him personally --- implicitly flattering the recipient as a man of sound judgment.
